808	\subsection{The affine base-change lemma and its remaining obligations}
809	
810	The affine base-change lemma below proceeds \emph{directly on global sections}: it
811	reduces, via the locality criterion
812	Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} and the two affine dictionaries
813	Lemmas~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso},
814	to two obligations that are isolated here as named blocks. Both are extracted from
815	the proof of Stacks ``Affine base change'', and both are absent from the pinned
816	Mathlib. The first is the \emph{locality reduction}: restricting to the affine opens
817	of \(S'\) reduces the isomorphism claim to the affine--affine model
818	\(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\),
819	\(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\). The second is the
820	\emph{section-level identification}: in that affine--affine model, the global-sections
821	incarnation \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) of the base-change map, transported through the two
822	proved tilde dictionaries, is the canonical cancellation isomorphism
823	\(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) — an isomorphism with no flatness hypothesis. This
824	is the honest cut of the affine close: locality on \(S'\), then a pure computation on
825	the module of global sections. No separate abstract adjoint-mate identification is
826	performed at the level of sheaves; the mate is unwound \emph{on sections}, where it
827	becomes \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) directly.
828	
829	\begin{lemma}
830	[Affine-local compatibility of the base-change map]
831	  \label{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}
832	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.base_change_map_affine_local}
833	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
834	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
835	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
836	  % "local on $S$ and $S'$" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
837	  % L920--926).
838	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
839	  % assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
840	  % $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
841	  % for some $A$-module $M$."
842	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
843	  change'' (proof, local-on-\(S\)-and-\(S'\) step).}
844	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
845	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, with a base-change square as above. The formation of
846	  the base-change map \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
847	  (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is compatible with restriction
848	  to affine opens of \(S'\): for each affine open \(U \subseteq S'\), the
849	  restriction of the base-change map to sections over \(U\) agrees with the
850	  base-change map of the square obtained by restricting along \(U\). Consequently,
851	  by the affine-open locality criterion
852	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the assertion that the base-change
853	  map is an isomorphism over an arbitrary base-change square \((S, S', X, X')\)
854	  reduces to the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
855	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
856	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\).
857	
858	  \medskip
859	  \emph{Intended formalized signature.} With the ambient data
860	  \(f : X \to S\), \(g : S' \to S\), \(f' : X' \to S'\), \(g' : X' \to X\) and a
861	  proof \(h\) that the square is cartesian (\(h : \mathrm{IsPullback}\ g'\ f'\ f\ g\),
862	  whose commutativity \(h.w : g' \circ f = f' \circ g\) feeds the base-change map),
863	  under the hypotheses \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) and
864	  \([\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}]\), the lemma is the locality reduction
865	  \[
866	    \bigl(\forall\, U : S'.\mathrm{affineOpens},\
867	      \mathrm{IsIso}\ \bigl((\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\ f\ g\ f'\ g'\ h.w\
868	        \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{app}\ U\bigr)\bigr)
869	    \;\Longrightarrow\;
870	    \mathrm{IsIso}\ \bigl(\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\ f\ g\ f'\ g'\ h.w\
871	      \mathcal{F}\bigr),
872	  \]
873	  i.e.\ it discharges the affine-open locality criterion
874	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} for the base-change map, with the
875	  per-affine-open hypothesis being exactly the affine--affine section assertion that
876	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange} supplies. The content
877	  beyond the bare locality criterion is the identification of the restriction of
878	  \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap} to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R')
879	  \subseteq S'\) with the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out over a
880	  chosen affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing \(g(U)\), over which
881	  \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) makes \(X\) restrict to \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
882	  \([\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}]\) makes \(\mathcal{F}\) restrict to
883	  \(\widetilde{M}\).
884	\end{lemma}
885	
886	\begin{proof}
887	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
888	  Being an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules is, by
889	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, a property that may be checked on
890	  sections over the affine opens of \(S'\). Restricting the cartesian square along
891	  an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R') \subseteq S'\) produces a cartesian
892	  square over \(U\) whose total space is the corresponding open of \(X'\). Choose an
893	  affine open \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing the image \(g(U)\);
894	  since affineness of \(f\) is preserved by base change and is local on the base, the
895	  total space \(X\) restricts over \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\) to an affine
896	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and \(\mathcal{F}\) restricts to a tilde-module
897	  \(\widetilde{M}\) there (this is where quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) is used).
898	  The base-change map of the restricted square is again
899	  \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}, now in the affine--affine model; the section
900	  functor over \(U\) carries the original base-change map to this restricted one,
901	  because \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap} is built from the
902	  \((\text{pullback} \dashv \text{pushforward})\) units and counits, all of which
903	  commute with restriction to an open. Granting this base-change-of-the-base-change-map
904	  compatibility — itself absent from the pinned Mathlib and recorded here as the named
905	  obligation — the assertion that \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap} is an isomorphism
906	  reduces, affine open by affine open, to the affine--affine case, where it is the
907	  section-level statement of
908	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}.
909	\end{proof}
910	
911	\begin{lemma}
912	[Section-level value of the base-change map equals the cancellation isomorphism]
913	  \label{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}
914	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}
915	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso,
916	  lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
917	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
918	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
919	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
920	  % L927--938).
921	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
922	  % to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
923	  % Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
924	  % $\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
925	  % to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$.
926	  % Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
927	  % equality
928	  % $$
929	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
930	  % $$
931	  % as $R'$-modules."
932	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
933	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
934	  In the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
935	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
936	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) — with \(g = \operatorname{Spec}(\psi)\) for
937	  \(\psi : R \to R'\), \(f = \operatorname{Spec}(\varphi)\) for \(\varphi : R \to A\),
938	  and \(M\) an \(A\)-module — write \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) for the global-sections
939	  incarnation of the base-change map \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
940	  (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}). Recall \(\alpha\) runs
941	  \(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M}) \to f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M}\). Transport \(\Gamma(\alpha)\)
942	  through the two proved affine dictionaries that identify its domain and codomain: the
943	  pushforward dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}, followed by the
944	  pullback dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, identifies the domain
945	  \(\Gamma\bigl(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M})\bigr)\) with \(R' \otimes_R M\); the pullback
946	  dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, followed by the pushforward
947	  dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}, identifies the codomain
948	  \(\Gamma\bigl(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M}\bigr)\) with \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\),
949	  both read as \(R'\)-modules. Under these identifications \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is
950	  precisely the canonical cancellation isomorphism
951	  \[
952	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
953	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M,
954	    \qquad (r' \otimes a) \otimes m \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m)
955	  \]
956	  (Mathlib's \texttt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}; here
957	  \(a \cdot m\) is the \(A\)-action on \(M\)). Equivalently, in the direction in which
958	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is presented — from the source \(R' \otimes_R M\) to the target
959	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) — it is \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}\),
960	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\); the two readings agree because
961	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism. Since
962	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with \emph{no} flatness
963	  hypothesis, this identification closes the affine case: \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is an
964	  isomorphism, hence (full faithfulness / counit isomorphism of \(\widetilde{(-)}\) on
965	  quasi-coherent modules) so is \(\alpha\) on the affine chart.
966	
967	  \medskip
968	  \emph{Intended formalized signature.} In the Lean affine--affine model — the square
969	  of \(\operatorname{Spec}\) maps induced by \(\psi : R \to R'\) and
970	  \(\varphi : R \to A\), with \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\),
971	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), and \(h\) the cartesian-square witness — the
972	  statement is the equality of \(R'\)-linear maps
973	  \[
974	    \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} \circ \Gamma\bigl(\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\
975	      f\ g\ f'\ g'\ h.w\ \widetilde{M}\bigr) \circ \Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1}
976	    \;=\;
977	    \bigl(\texttt{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}\bigr)^{-1},
978	  \]
979	  where \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}} : \Gamma\bigl(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M})\bigr)
980	  \xrightarrow{\sim} R' \otimes_R M\) is assembled from
981	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} and
982	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, and
983	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} : \Gamma\bigl(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M}\bigr)
984	  \xrightarrow{\sim} (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) is assembled from the same two
985	  dictionaries; equivalently, after pre/post-composing with the dictionary isos,
986	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) \emph{is} the cancellation isomorphism. The conclusion type is a
987	  propositional equality of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\)-morphisms (whence
988	  \(\mathrm{IsIso}\ \Gamma(\alpha)\) and \(\mathrm{IsIso}\ \alpha\) follow), not merely
989	  an abstract-mate identification at the level of sheaves.
990	\end{lemma}
991	
992	\begin{proof}
993	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}
994	  The base-change map \(\alpha = \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\) is the transpose,
995	  under the \((\text{pullback} \dashv \text{pushforward})\) adjunction for \(g\), of
996	  the composite
997	  \[
998	    f_*\widetilde{M}
999	    \xrightarrow{\;f_*(\eta')\;} f_*(g')_*(g')^*\widetilde{M}
1000	    = (g' f)_*(g')^*\widetilde{M}
1001	    = (f' g)_*(g')^*\widetilde{M}
1002	    = g_* f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M},
1003	  \]
1004	  where \(\eta'\) is the unit of the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction and the middle
1005	  equalities are the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward and the commutativity of the
1006	  square. We compute \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) on the module of global sections, tracing a
1007	  generator and naming the adjunction unit/counit expanded at each step. Throughout we
1008	  use the two affine dictionaries to read each functor on modules:
1009	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-pushforward is restriction of scalars
1010	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}) and \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-pullback is
1011	  extension of scalars (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}). On global sections,
1012	  \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), so \((g')^*\widetilde{M}\) reads as the
1013	  \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\).
1014	
1015	  \medskip
1016	  \emph{Step 1: the \(((g')^*,(g')_*)\)-unit \(\eta'\).} Under the dictionaries,
1017	  \((g')^* = (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A (-)\) (extension of scalars along
1018	  \(\theta : A \to R' \otimes_R A\)) and \((g')_*\) is restriction of scalars back
1019	  along \(\theta\). The unit \(\eta'_{\widetilde M}\) on global \(A\)-modules is the
1020	  base-change unit
1021	  \[
1022	    M \longrightarrow (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M, \qquad
1023	    m \longmapsto 1 \otimes m = (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m,
1024	  \]
1025	  i.e.\ \(m \mapsto 1 \otimes m\) into the extension of scalars, read back as an
1026	  \(A\)-linear map into the restriction of scalars.
1027	
1028	  \medskip
1029	  \emph{Step 2: apply \(f_* = \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\) and read off the
1030	  pseudofunctor identifications.} Applying \(f_*\) (restriction of scalars along
1031	  \(\varphi : R \to A\)) turns \(\eta'\) into the same assignment

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
46	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
47	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
48	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
49	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
50	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
51	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
52	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
53	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
54	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
55	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
56	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
57	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
58	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
59	
60	  The intended formal target (currently pinned to the placeholder
61	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}) regards \(M\) as an
62	  \(A\)-module through the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\), and packages the
63	  localisation \(M_f\) as the localised module at the powers of \(f\): there exists
64	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \texttt{LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M}
65	  is free over \texttt{Localization.Away f}.
66	\end{theorem}
67	
68	% INTENDED LEAN SIGNATURE (for the scaffold/refactor pass; the \lean{} pin above
69	% remains the placeholder TODO.genericFlatnessAlgebraic until then):
70	%   (A B M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
71	%     [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Algebra.FiniteType A B]
72	%     [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
73	%     [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
74	%     ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
75	%       Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M)
76	
77	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
78	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
79	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
80	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
81	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
82	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
83	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
84	% there exists a finite filtration
85	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
86	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
87	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
88	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
89	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
90	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
91	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
92	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case as a thin Mathlib wrapper.}
96	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself (i.e.\ \(M\) is a finite
97	  \(A\)-module, not merely a finite \(B\)-module). Since \(A\) is noetherian, a finite
98	  \(A\)-module is finitely presented over \(A\). Let \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) be the
99	  localisation of \(A\) at the multiplicative set \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\). The
100	  generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M = S^{-1} M\) is a \emph{finite-dimensional}
101	  \(K\)-vector space, hence free over \(K\); thus the freeness hypothesis at the
102	  generic localisation is automatic, with no input from the structure of \(B\). The
103	  result is then exactly Mathlib's
104	  \texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers}
105	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Localization.Free}): a finitely-presented \(A\)-module
106	  that is free over the localisation \(A_S = K\) becomes free over
107	  \texttt{Localization.Away}~\(r\) after inverting some \(r \in S\), i.e.\ some
108	  \(r \in A\), \(r \ne 0\). Setting \(f := r\) gives \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). (The
109	  openness of the free locus is the \texttt{Module.freeLocus} API of
110	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Spectrum.Prime.FreeLocus}, which records that the locus
111	  where \(M\) is free over \(A\) is open and, by the above, non-empty.) This closes
112	  the theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
113	
114	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
115	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
116	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
117	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
118	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
119	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
120	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
121	  for exactly this residue.
122	
123	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
124	  Pass to the fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) and
125	  set \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\), \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\), \(n := \dim \mathrm{supp}_{\Spec B_K} M_K\).
126	  Argue by induction on \(n\), with base case \(n = -1\) (i.e.\ \(M_K = 0\)): each
127	  generator of \(M\) is annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\); taking the
128	  product of finitely many annihilators yields \(f \in A\) with \(f M = 0\), hence
129	  \(M_f = 0\) is trivially free.  For \(n \ge 0\), a prime filtration of \(M\) as a
130	  finite \(B\)-module
131	  \[
132	    0 = M_0 \subset M_1 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M
133	  \]
134	  with successive quotients \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B / \mathfrak{p}_i\) reduces the
135	  problem (via the splicing fact: \(M_{f'f''}\) is \(A_{f'f''}\)-free when \(M'_{f'}\)
136	  and \(M''_{f''}\) are free) to the case \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) for a prime
137	  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset B\), then to the case \(B\) a domain and \(M = B\).
138	  Noether normalisation applied to \(B_K / K\) yields algebraically independent
139	  \(b_1, \dots, b_n \in B\) with \(B_K\) finite over \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); choosing
140	  \(g \ne 0\) to clear denominators of integrality equations, \(B_g\) is finite over
141	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Let \(m\) be the generic rank: the short exact sequence
142	  \[
143	    0 \to A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0
144	  \]
145	  has \(T\) a finite torsion module over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), so $\dim
146	  \mathrm{supp}_{B_K} (K \otimes_{A_g} T) < n$.  The inductive hypothesis
147	  yields \(h \ne 0\) in \(A\) with \(T_h\) free over \(A_{gh}\); set \(f := gh\).
148	  At the bottom of this induction the module \(B_g\) is finite over the polynomial
149	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), so each torsion-free graded piece is — after a
150	  further inversion — finite over \(A_g\); this is the point at which the residue
151	  meets the finite \(A\)-module case discharged by the primary route above.
152	\end{proof}
153	
154	\begin{theorem}
155	
156	\leanok
157	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
158	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
160	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
161	  % NOTE: the Lean signature `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness` currently takes
162	  % `F : X.Modules` with NO coherence hypothesis, so it is FALSE as stated: a
163	  % non-coherent module sheaf need not become flat on any non-empty open. Its
164	  % `sorry` is therefore unprovable until the signature is corrected. The decl is
165	  % NOT in archon-protected.yaml, so it must be re-signed (a scaffold/refactor pass)
166	  % before any prover is sent at it.
167	  %
168	  % Mathlib has no single `IsCoherent` predicate at this pin; coherence over a
169	  % locally noetherian base is encoded as the conjunction of two existing
170	  % SheafOfModules predicates on `F`:
171	  %     [F.IsQuasicoherent]   (SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent,
172	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent)
173	  %     [F.IsFiniteType]      (SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType,
174	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Generators)
175	  %
176	  % CORRECTED INTENDED LEAN SIGNATURE (faithful target for the re-sign pass):
177	  %   theorem genericFlatness {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
178	  %       (p : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType p] (F : X.Modules)
179	  %       [F.IsQuasicoherent] [F.IsFiniteType] :
180	  %       ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧ … (flatness conclusion unchanged)
181	
182	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
183	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
184	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
185	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
186	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
187	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
188	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
189	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
190	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
191	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
192	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
193	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
194	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
195	
196	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: without it the conclusion is
197	  false, since a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module need not become flat over any
198	  non-empty open of \(S\). The intended formal target therefore carries \(\F\)
199	  coherent over the locally noetherian base \(S\), which — absent a single
200	  \texttt{IsCoherent} predicate in Mathlib at this pin — is encoded as the
201	  conjunction of the two module-sheaf hypotheses \(\F\) quasi-coherent
202	  (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent}) and \(\F\) of finite type
203	  (\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType}), together with \(p\) locally of finite type
204	  and \(S\) integral and locally noetherian.
205	\end{theorem}
206	
207	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
208	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
209	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
210	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
211	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
212	% by restricting to a non-empty
213	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
214	\begin{proof}
215	  
216	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(\Spec A \subseteq S\) where \(A\) is a
217	  noetherian domain. The morphism \(p\) is of finite type, so locally on \(X\)
218	  above this affine \(\F\) is the sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) for \(M\) a finite module
219	  over a finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\). \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
220	  produces \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\) on each such patch;
221	  shrinking to the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec A\) globalises by patching
222	  finitely many such opens (using noetherianity of \(X\) above the affine).
223	\end{proof}
224	
225	\section{Mathlib status}
226	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
227	
228	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
229	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
230	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
231	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
232	generic-flatness argument consumes
233	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
234	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
235	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
236	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
237	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
238	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
239	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
240	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
241	are introduced in
242	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}; the
243	file-internal \texttt{GenericFreeness} namespace already lands the
244	finite-module / finite-morphism special case axiom-clean.
245

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
62	  % Polynomials" (read from
63	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
64	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
65	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
66	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
67	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
68	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
69	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
70	  %  the function
71	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
72	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
73	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
74	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
75	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
76	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
77	  %
78	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
79	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
80	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
81	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
82	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
83	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
84	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
85	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
86	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
87	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
88	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
89	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
90	  III.5.2.}
91	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
92	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
93	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), the
94	  \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\) relative to \(L\)} is the
95	  polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) characterised by
96	  \[
97	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
98	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
99	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
100	  \]
101	  (a polynomial in \(m\) by Snapper's Lemma; the sum is finite by coherence and
102	  properness). When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
103	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
104	  well-defined on connected components.
105	\end{definition}
106	
107	\section{The Quot functor}
108	\label{sec:quot_functor}
109	
110	\begin{definition}\leanok
111	[Quot functor]
112	  \label{def:quot_functor}
113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
114	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial}
115	
116	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
117	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
118	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
119	  % + L481-L498).
120	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
121	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
122	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
123	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
124	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
125	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
126	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
127	  %
128	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
129	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
130	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
131	  %
132	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
133	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
134	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
135	  %
136	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
137	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
138	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
139	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
140	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
141	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
142	  %  well-defined, which gives
143	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
144	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
145	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
146	  %  [...]
147	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
148	  %  as a co-product
149	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
150	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
151	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
152	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
153	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
154	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
155	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
156	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
157	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
158	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
159	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
160	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
161	  \[
162	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
163	  \]
164	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
165	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
166	  \begin{enumerate}
167	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
168	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) and which is flat over \(T\);
169	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
170	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
171	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
172	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
173	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
174	      \(\Phi\).
175	  \end{enumerate}
176	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
177	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
178	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
179	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
180	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
181	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
182	\end{definition}
183	
184	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
185	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
186	
187	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
188	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
189	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
190	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
191	
192	\begin{definition}\leanok
193	[Grassmannian scheme]
194	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
195	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
196	  \uses{def:quot_functor}
197	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
198	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
199	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
200	  % + L773-L850).
201	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
202	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
203	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
204	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
205	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
206	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
207	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
208	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
209	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
210	  %  contravariant functor
211	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
212	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
213	  %  set of all equivalence classes
214	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
215	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
216	  %  [...]
217	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
218	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
219	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
220	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
221	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
222	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
223	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
224	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
225	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
226	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
227	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
228	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
229	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
230	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
231	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
232	  \[
233	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
234	    \qquad
235	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
236	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
237	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
238	  \]
239	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Concretely \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) =
240	  \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\) (the Quot functor for \(X = S\), \(E = V\),
241	  constant Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\)).
242	
243	  Structurally \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d)\) is \emph{defined} as this Quot functor
244	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) of the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\),
245	  \(\Phi = d\) -- not as a second, independently-built functor. Fixing the
246	  definition this way makes the identity
247	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\) hold by
248	  construction, so the structural correspondence is preserved automatically
249	  when the underlying \(\Quot\) construction is later filled in, and the
250	  Grassmannian inherits its functoriality and equivalence relation from the
251	  Quot functor rather than restating them.
252	\end{definition}
253	
254	\begin{lemma}[Representability predicate]
255	  \label{lem:functor_is_representable_mathlib}
256	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRepresentable}
257	  \mathlibok
258	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
259	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a category and \(F : \mathcal{C}^{op} \to \mathbf{Set}\)
260	  a presheaf of sets. The predicate \(F.\mathrm{IsRepresentable}\) asserts that
261	  \(F\) is representable. It unfolds -- via
262	  \texttt{Functor.IsRepresentable.has\_representation} -- to exactly
263	  \[
264	    \exists\,Y,\;\; \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(F.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\,Y\bigr),
265	  \]
266	  i.e.\ the existence of an object \(Y\) of \(\mathcal{C}\) together with a
267	  natural bijection \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(-, Y) \cong F\). When it holds,
268	  \texttt{Functor.representableBy} produces the canonical witness
269	  \(F.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\,(F.\mathrm{reprX})\) on the representing object
270	  \(F.\mathrm{reprX}\).
271	\end{lemma}
272	
273	\begin{theorem}\leanok
274	[Representability of the Grassmannian]
275	  \label{thm:grassmannian_representable}
276	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian.representable}
277	  \uses{def:grassmannian_scheme, thm:relative_spec_exists, thm:relative_spec_univ,
278	    lem:functor_is_representable_mathlib}
279	
280	  % NOTE (representability proof blocked): the sketch below recovers the
281	  % representing object through the RepresentableBy Yoneda form "as in the
282	  % RelativeSpec chapter" (\cref{thm:relative_spec_univ}). But
283	  % thm:relative_spec_univ is currently established at the Lean level only as
284	  % "IsAffineHom (structureMorphism A)", which is strictly weaker than a
285	  % CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy witness. The PROOF of
286	  % thm:grassmannian_representable is therefore blocked on either (a)
287	  % strengthening thm:relative_spec_univ to deliver a RepresentableBy witness,
288	  % or (b) finding a RepresentableBy-free Grassmannian argument (gluing the
289	  % chart-wise classifying morphisms into the representing object directly).
290	  % This is a deferred open question, not resolved in the present pass; the
291	  % RelativeSpec chapter is not edited here.
292	
293	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
294	  % affine patches" through "Projective embedding"
295	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
296	  % L798-L930).
297	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
298	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), the Grassmannian scheme
299	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
300	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
301	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
302	  %  can be explicitly constructed as follows. [...]
303	  %  the schemes
304	  %  \(U^I\), as \(I\) varies over all the
305	  %  \({r\choose d}\) different
306	  %  subsets of  \(\{ 1,\ldots, r\}\) of cardinality \(d\),
307	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle \((\theta_{I,J})\) to form a
308	  %  finite-type scheme \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\). As each \(U^I\) is
309	  %  isomorphic to \(\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}\), it follows that
310	  %  \(\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is smooth of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\).
311	  %  [...]
312	  %  We now show that \(\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is proper. It is enough to
313	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
314	  %  [...]
315	  %  As \(\U\) is given by the transition functions \(g_{I,J}\) described above,
316	  %  the determinant line bundle
317	  %  \(\det(\U)\) is given by the transition functions
318	  %  \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J \in GL_1(U^I_J)\).
319	  %  [...]
320	  %  We leave it to the reader to verify that the sections \(\sigma_I\) form
321	  %  a linear system which is base point free and separates points
322	  %  relative to \(\spec \Z\), and so gives an embedding of
323	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) into \(\PP^m_{\Z}\) where \(m = {r\choose d}-1\).
324	  %  This is a closed embedding by the properness of
325	  %  \(\pi:\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\). In particular, \(\det(\U)\)
326	  %  is a relatively very ample line bundle on \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\)."
327	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Construction of Grassmannian" (FGA
328	  Explained Ch.~5).}
329	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
330	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
331	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
332	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
333	  equipped with a tautological quotient
334	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
335	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
336	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
337	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
338	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)\) -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.
339	
340	  \emph{Faithful target.} The representability conclusion is exactly Mathlib's
341	  representability predicate (\cref{lem:functor_is_representable_mathlib}): the
342	  intended statement is \((\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)).\mathrm{IsRepresentable}\), whose
343	  unfolding \texttt{Functor.IsRepresentable.has\_representation} is precisely
344	  \(\exists\,Y : \mathrm{Over}\,S,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)).\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\,Y\bigr)\).
345	  Stating the theorem in the \texttt{IsRepresentable} spelling lets the parent
346	  project recover the representing \(S\)-scheme and the Yoneda bijection
347	  directly through \texttt{Functor.representableBy} / \texttt{Functor.reprX} at
348	  merge-back, instead of re-bridging an ad-hoc existential.
349	
350	  \emph{Universe constraint.} \(\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\,F\,Y\) is available
351	  only for \(F : \mathcal{C}^{op} \to \mathrm{Type}\,v\) whose representing
352	  object \(Y\) lies in the \emph{same} category \(\mathcal{C}\) at the
353	  \emph{matching} hom-universe \(v\). Hence the target universe of the
354	  Grassmannian functor (and of the Quot functor of \cref{def:quot_functor} on
355	  which it is built) must be pinned to the hom-universe of the schemes category
356	  \(\mathrm{Sch}/S\) -- equivalently of \(\mathrm{Over}\,S\), in which the
357	  representing object \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) lives. If the functor's value
358	  universe is left free, the representability statement does not typecheck.
359	\end{theorem}
360	
361	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
362	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
363	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
364	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
365	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
366	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
367	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
368	\begin{proof}
369	  
370	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
371	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
372	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
373	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
374	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
375	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
376	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
377	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
378	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
379	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
380	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
381	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
382	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
383	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
384	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
385	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
386	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
387	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
388	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
389	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
390	
391	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
392	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
393	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
394	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
395	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
396	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
397	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
398	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
399	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
400	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
401	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
402	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
403	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
404	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
405	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
406	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
407	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
408	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
409	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
410	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
411	\end{proof}
412	
413	\section{Lean encoding}
414	\label{sec:quot_lean_encoding}
415	
416	The Lean target file is
417	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean}. The Quot-foundations
418	layer proceeds in two phases.
419	
420	\paragraph{Phase 1: Hilbert polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}).}
421	On a finite-type morphism \(\pi : X \to S\) with \(S\) noetherian and a
422	coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) with proper support over \(S\), the per-fiber
423	Hilbert polynomial is encoded as a function \(s \mapsto
424	\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). Mathlib provides
425	Euler-characteristic infrastructure on coherent sheaves on noetherian
426	schemes; the polynomial property of
427	\(m \mapsto \chi(\mathcal{F} \otimes L^{\otimes m})\) requires
428	Snapper's Lemma, which is a project-side sub-build on top of the
429	graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure.
430	
431	\emph{Intended signature of \(\mathrm{hilbertPolynomial}\).} Mathlib at the pin
432	carries no single \texttt{IsCoherent} predicate; coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\)
433	over the locally noetherian base is the conjunction of the two predicates that
434	do exist, \texttt{SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent} and
435	\texttt{SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType} -- i.e.\ the hypotheses
436	\([\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}]\) and
437	\([\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsFiniteType}]\) (and likewise for the line bundle
438	\(L\)). The properness-of-support hypothesis
439	``\(\operatorname{supp}\mathcal{F}\) proper over \(S\)'' has \emph{no} clean
440	Mathlib predicate. The minimal honest encoding forms the schematic support of
441	\(\mathcal{F}\) as a closed subscheme \(Z \hookrightarrow X\) and requires the
442	composite \(Z \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{\pi} S\) to satisfy
443	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}; since the schematic-support closed
444	subscheme of a coherent sheaf is not itself available at the pin, this support
445	datum is recorded as a project-side gap to be supplied alongside the
446	definition. (The existing Lean pin
447	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial} is retained; adding these
448	hypotheses to its signature is a later refactor of the \texttt{.lean} stub, not
449	done in this pass.)
450	
451	\paragraph{Phase 2: Quot and Grassmannian functors
452	(\cref{def:quot_functor}, \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme},
453	\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).} The Quot functor
454	\(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) is encoded as a contravariant functor on
455	\((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\) of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients; the Grassmannian
456	\(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d)\) is the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\), constant
457	Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\). The representing scheme
458	\(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) is the relative gluing of \(\binom{r}{d}\)
459	affine charts \(U^I\) (each isomorphic to a relative affine space
460	\(\mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_S\)) via the Pl\"ucker cocycle \(\theta_{I,J}\). The
461	gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData}. The
462	universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) is glued by the transition cocycle
463	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the determinant line bundle
464	\(\det \mathcal{U}\) gives the Pl\"ucker closed embedding into
465	\(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\). The representability statement is pinned to
466	Mathlib's \texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRepresentable}
467	(\cref{lem:functor_is_representable_mathlib}), which packages exactly the
468	\texttt{RepresentableBy} witness on the universal pair
469	\((\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d), \mathcal{U})\), in the same Yoneda style as
470	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
471	
472	\emph{Intended signatures.} Coherence of \(E\) in \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) is
473	encoded as in Phase 1, by the conjunction \([E.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}]\) and
474	\([E.\mathrm{IsFiniteType}]\) over the locally noetherian base (no single
475	\texttt{IsCoherent} predicate exists at the pin). For \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d)\),
476	the hypothesis ``\(V\) locally free of rank \(r\)'' has no faithful Mathlib
477	predicate at the pin: \texttt{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFree} appears only
478	upstream of the pinned commit and is in any case rank-agnostic (it fixes no
479	value \(r\)). The intended encoding therefore pairs an
480	\texttt{IsLocallyFree}-style hypothesis on \(V\) with an explicit rank
481	\(r : \mathbb{N}\) and the constraint \(1 \le d \le r\) carried as a hypothesis
482	on naturals; the rank-\(r\) local-freeness predicate is flagged as a
483	project-side gap to be supplied. Consistently with
484	\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}, \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d)\) is \emph{defined} as
485	\(\Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\) (the case \(X = S\), \(E = V\),
486	\(\Phi = d\)) rather than as an independent functor. The existing Lean pins on
487	\(\Quot\) and \(\mathrm{Grass}\) are retained; threading these hypotheses
488	through the \texttt{.lean} signatures is a later refactor.
489	
490	\section{Out of scope}
491	\label{sec:quot_out_of_scope}
492	
493	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Quot-foundations layer: the Hilbert
494	polynomial, the Quot functor, and the Grassmannian scheme with its
495	representability. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
496	\begin{itemize}
497	  \item \textbf{Representability of the Quot scheme} -- the
498	    Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman existence theorem and its proof skeleton
499	    (boundedness, Grassmannian embedding, flattening stratification,
500	    valuative criterion) are not constructed in this chapter.
501	  \item \textbf{Flat base change of cohomology} (Stacks 02KH) and its
502	    project-side substrate -- not constructed in this chapter.
503	  \item \textbf{Hilbert scheme as a separate chapter}. The Hilbert scheme
504	    \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) is the special case
505	    \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\) of the Quot functor and is
506	    introduced inline (\cref{def:quot_functor}); no separate chapter.
507	  \item \textbf{Snapper's Lemma} -- the polynomial-eventually property of
508	    Euler characteristics, used in \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Cited
509	    via Nitsure \S 1 and (textbook reference) Hartshorne III.5.2;
510	    project-side sub-build for the Lean encoding.
511	  \item \textbf{Identification with \(\mathbb{P}^n\) and relative
512	    Grassmannian variants} (Nitsure \S 1, Exercises (1)--(4)) --
513	    optional applications, not consumed by Route A.
514	  \item \textbf{Group-scheme structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)} (A.2.c) -- the
515	    abelian-group-scheme refinement is downstream. Its blueprint home is the
516	    FGA Picard-representability assembly (the chapter that bundles the relative
517	    Picard functor, its representability, and the abelian-group-scheme
518	    structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)); that assembly lives in the parent project
519	    which consumes this Quot-foundations layer and is not part of the present
520	    subproject.
521	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety
522	    structure} (A.3) -- a separate Route A sub-row, fed by but not
523	    constructed in this chapter.
524	\end{itemize}

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60	
61	% NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-174 carrier note. The iter-179
62	% Block A refactor (analogies/relative-spec-encoding.md) upgrades the carrier
63	% to a 3-field structure

65	%     `  sheaf        : TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat.{u} X.toPresheafedSpace`
66	%     `  unit         : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf`
67	%     `  coequifibered : NatTrans.Coequifibered`
68	%     `    (Functor.whiskerLeft (AffineZariskiSite.toOpensFunctor X).op unit.hom)`
69	% The new third field IS the iter-175+ overlay the prior note flagged: Mathlib
70	% commit `b80f227`'s Stacks-01LL `NatTrans.Coequifibered` predicate is strictly
71	% weaker than the full `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` claimed by Definition
72	% 1.1's prose but is equivalent for the geometric purpose (constructing the
73	% relative-spectrum scheme) via the dense-subsite equivalence
74	% `AffineZariskiSite.sheafEquiv`. This is the predicate Mathlib's
75	% `AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData` consumes, so the iter-179
76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}
104	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{
109	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
111	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
112	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).
136	\end{theorem}
137	
138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}

160	\begin{definition}
161	
162	\leanok
163	[Structure morphism of the relative spectrum]
164	  \label{def:relspec_structure_morphism}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.structureMorphism}
166	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
167	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
168	  The \emph{structure morphism}
169	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) is the canonical projection of the
170	  relative spectrum to its base, obtained as the colimit descent of the affine-local
171	  structure morphisms \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \to U\) over the directed affine open
172	  cover of \(X\). It is the morphism whose affineness and base-change behaviour are the
173	  subject of the universal-property and base-change results below.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\begin{proof}
177	
178	\leanok
179	  Constructed directly in Lean as the base morphism of the relative gluing data of
180	  \(\mathcal{A}\); this is the projection underlying \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
181	\end{proof}

183	\begin{theorem}\leanok
184	[Universal property of the relative spectrum]
185	  \label{thm:relative_spec_univ}
186	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty}
187	  % NOTE iter-283: removed the spurious `thm:relative_spec_base_change` from this
188	  % \uses. The universal property is the PRIMITIVE statement, proved by reducing
189	  % to the affine-base case and gluing (Lean body of `UniversalProperty` uses

194	  % `leanblueprint web`.
195	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, thm:relative_spec_affine_base,
196	    def:relspec_structure_morphism}

199	  % structural consequence ``IsAffineHom (structureMorphism 𝒜)'' rather than
200	  % the natural-bijection statement pinned by this prose. Iter-174+ must

202	  % witness (or equivalent) before any consumer file relies on this theorem.
203	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ continuation: Section ``Relative spectrum as
204	  % a functor'', functor F and Lemma lemma-spec
205	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L427-L466 + L547-L551).
206	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
207	  % "For any \(f : T \to S\) the pullback
208	  %  \(f^*\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-algebras.
209	  %  We are going to consider pairs \((f : T \to S, \varphi)\) where
210	  %  \(f\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\varphi : f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\)
211	  %  is a morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-algebras. Note that this is the
212	  %  same as giving a \(f^{-1}\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra homomorphism
213	  %  \(\varphi : f^{-1}\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\), see
214	  %  Sheaves, Lemma REF.
215	  %  This is also the same as giving an \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra map
216	  %  \(\varphi : \mathcal{A} \to f_*\mathcal{O}_T\), see
217	  %  Sheaves, Lemma REF.
218	  %  We will use all three ways of thinking about \(\varphi\),
219	  %  without further mention. [...]
220	  %  In this way we have defined a functor
221	  %  \begin{eqnarray}
222	  %  \label{equation-spec}
223	  %  F : \Sch^{opp} & \longrightarrow & \textit{Sets} \\
224	  %  T & \longmapsto & F(T) = \{\text{pairs }(f, \varphi) \text{ as above}\}
225	  %  \nonumber
226	  %  \end{eqnarray}
227	  %  [...]
228	  %  The functor \(F\) is representable by a scheme."
229	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-spec + section-spec
230	  functor description); [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
231	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
232	  Then \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) represents the functor
233	  \(F : \Sch^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\),
234	  $T \mapsto F(T) := \{(f, \varphi) : f : T \to X \text{ a morphism},\;
235	  \varphi : f^* \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T \text{ an } \mathcal{O}_T\text{-algebra map}\}.$
236	  Equivalently, for any \(X\)-scheme \(g : T \to X\),
237	  there is a natural bijection
238	  \[

240	    \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_* \mathcal{O}_T)
241	  \]
242	  (using the adjunction between \(g_*\) and \(g^*\) to convert between
243	  \(\mathcal{A} \to g_*\mathcal{O}_T\) and \(g^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\)).
244	  The bijection is natural in \(T\).
245	\end{theorem}

247	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: TODO retrieve from references/stacks-constructions.tex
248	% (proof of lemma-spec, L553-L600, ~50 lines covering the Zariski sheaf property,
249	% the subfunctors F_i associated to an affine cover, and identifying each F_i
250	% with the affine-base case via lemma-spec-base-change and lemma-spec-affine).
251	% Verbatim quote omitted for length; the structural steps are restated below in
252	% project notation.
253	\begin{proof}
254	  
255	  \leanok
256	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
257	  % The iter-179 Block A refactor replaces the iter-176 placeholder bodies with
258	  % the Mathlib values ``(AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData _).glued`` /
259	  % ``.toBase``; iter-179 Lane B closes the Lean ``UniversalProperty`` proof
260	  % via ``isAffineHom_of_forall_exists_isAffineOpen`` +
261	  % ``relativeGluingData.toBase_preimage_eq_opensRange_ι`` +
262	  % ``isAffineOpen_opensRange`` — a real proof against the gluing infrastructure,
263	  % NOT against a trivial identity placeholder. The Lean type remains the
264	  % strictly weaker ``IsAffineHom (structureMorphism 𝒜)'' rather than the
265	  % Yoneda-bijection form pinned above (iter-174+ commitment; still pending
266	  % iter-180+). Consumers may now rely on the affineness conclusion, but the
267	  % Yoneda-bijection witness is still deferred.
268	  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor \(F\) is a Zariski sheaf
269	  because morphisms of schemes and \(\mathcal{O}\)-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
270	  Choose an affine open cover \(X = \bigcup U_i\). The subfunctor
271	  \(F_i \subseteq F\) of pairs \((f, \varphi)\) with \(f(T) \subseteq U_i\) is representable by
272	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))\) via the affine-base case
273	  (\cref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) together with the
274	  restriction identification \(F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}\).
275	  Each \(F_i \hookrightarrow F\) is an open
276	  subfunctor (pull back \(U_i\) along \(f\)). The \(F_i\) jointly cover \(F\) since the \(U_i\)
277	  cover \(X\). The gluing of representable open subfunctors produces a representing scheme,
278	  which is canonically isomorphic to the glued scheme \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)
279	  of \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
280	\end{proof}
